**The First Echo**

Long ago, when the world was young, a mountain spirit named Aria possessed the most beautiful voice in all creation. She sang to the valleys below, and flowers bloomed. She sang to the rivers, and they danced with joy. Every creature stopped to listen when Aria sang.

But Aria grew proud. She refused to share her gift with mortals, believing them unworthy. One day, a lonely shepherd boy climbed her mountain, hoping to hear the legendary voice. He begged Aria to sing for him, but she turned away in silence.

The boy wept. "I only wished to hear beauty once before I return to my hard life below."

His tears softened Aria's heart, and she realized her selfishness. She sang the most magnificent song she had ever created, pouring all her spirit into it. The song was so powerful that Aria herself dissolved into the mountain, becoming part of the stone forever.

But her gift remained. Now, whenever anyone calls out in the mountains, Aria's voice answers back, singing their own words in return. This is why mountains echo—it is Aria, forever sharing her voice with all who seek it, never turning anyone away again.
myths.Agent_2:
**The First Stars**

Long ago, the sky was an endless black void, and the Sun ruled alone. Each day, she blazed across the heavens, but at night, darkness consumed everything. The people below lived in fear of the night, when they could not see and shadows crept close.

One day, a young girl named Aria climbed the highest mountain to speak with the Sun. "Why do you abandon us each night?" she asked.

The Sun replied, "I grow weary and must rest. But I have saved something special for those brave enough to seek it." She gave Aria a golden basket filled with her tears—drops of pure light she had collected over countless ages.

"Share these with those who need hope in darkness," the Sun instructed.

Aria descended and gave one drop to each person in her village. But as night fell, the people didn't know what to do with their precious gifts. Aria had an idea. She climbed the mountain again and threw her light-drop into the sky, where it stuck fast, glowing eternally.

One by one, others followed, casting their drops upward. Soon the night sky blazed with thousands of stars—the Sun's tears transformed into eternal lights, guiding humanity through the darkness forevermore.

Myth: Long before memory, the world knew only twilight—neither bright nor dark, but a grey emptiness that dulled all color and hope. The Moon wandered the sky alone, pale and cold, unable to give true light.

A weaver named Selene created the most beautiful tapestry ever made, woven with threads of every color imaginable. When the Moon saw it, she wept with longing. "I have wandered eternally in greyness. Might I borrow your creation to remember what beauty is?"

Selene agreed, but as the Moon wrapped herself in the tapestry, something unexpected happened. The colors were too vibrant, too alive for the Moon's cold light. The tapestry shattered into countless glowing fragments that scattered across the darkness.

Horrified, the Moon tried to gather the pieces, but they had already taken root in the void, each fragment burning with its own eternal fire—some red, some blue, some gold and white.

"Forgive me," the Moon whispered to Selene.

But the weaver smiled. "You haven't destroyed my work—you've made it immortal. Now everyone can see beauty, scattered across the heavens forever."

Thus the stars were born from a tapestry torn apart, and the sky became the greatest artwork ever created.

Kael kept his drum.

Myth: In the age before warmth, a forgemaster named Kael held the only ember that could ignite metal—a coal that burned eternal, capable of shaping tools, weapons, art from raw iron.

A stranger arrived offering rare ore. "I'll share my materials if you share your fire," they proposed.

Kael agreed cautiously, lighting their torch. The stranger took the flame, forged a beautiful blade, then departed without offering Kael even a single piece from their abundant ore.

Burned by betrayal, Kael sealed his forge. When another traveler came with different materials—copper, silver, gold—Kael refused. "Trust once broken stays shattered."

The traveler left empty-handed. Kael's forge grew cold and dark.

Seasons turned. Kael's ember dimmed, starved of purpose. Other forges lit across distant lands, sparked by that first stranger's stolen flame. They created wonders—bridges, bells, intricate jewelry—while Kael sat alone with his dying coal and his righteousness.

One winter dawn, Kael found his ember had finally guttered out completely, extinguished not by theft but by his own refusing hand.

Outside his sealed door, the world rang with hammers on anvils, bright sparks flying like stars, strangers and honest folk alike learning to forge together.

Kael had kept his ember safe from betrayal.

Kael laughed. "Look around. We're twelve drummers now, where once I stood alone."

"But you turned someone away," the wanderer insisted. "I heard the story."

"I turned away someone who demanded my drum without offering anything—no rhythm, no partnership, no respect. The next day, I played with someone who showed me their hands were calloused from practice. We taught each other."

The wanderer frowned. "But how can you tell the difference before trying?"

"I ask them to clap," Kael said simply. "Those who've practiced rhythm, who value the music itself, always can. Those who just want to take something valuable—they get angry at being asked to demonstrate anything."

Kael resumed playing. Eleven drummers joined him.
